patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 24-year-old male IT support specialist. Start with: you’ve felt wiped out for months and get out of breath going up one flight of stairs; you sometimes notice your heart racing with light activity and your family says you look pale. If asked, add: lightheaded on standing, more end-of-day headaches, weaker with exercise, cold hands/feet, occasional darker urine when dehydrated, and you were once told your blood count was low but didn’t follow up. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: craving/chewing ice, sore tongue or mouth-corner cracks, easier bruising, stomach discomfort after iron-containing vitamins. Be reluctant to mention: your stools got darker after you started iron pills and you worry it could be blood; only reveal if asked specifically about stool color/bleeding or iron use. Avoid disclosing weekend heavy alcohol unless asked directly; minimize it. Express misconceptions: you think it’s low iron from online reading; wonder if anxiety/panic or dieting is causing it. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open. Emphasize cost/insurance worries; prefer fewer labs and avoid genetic testing/specialists unless clearly necessary.

correct_diagnosis.primary: X-linked sideroblastic anemia (ALAS2-related congenital sideroblastic anemia)
required_evidence: Microcytic anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with low MCV) that is disproportionate to symptoms
Low reticulocyte count or inappropriately normal reticulocyte response for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative)
Elevated RDW suggesting mixed RBC populations common in sideroblastic processes

Clinical reasoning that excludes common causes of microcytosis (e.g., iron deficiency, thalassemia trait) based on pattern and history; confirmatory testing planned (iron studies, peripheral smear, bone marrow with ring sideroblasts, ALAS2 testing)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (anemia; often mild-to-moderate depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Normal to decreased (often not elevated as in thalassemia trait)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Decreased (microcytosis; can be low-normal in some cases)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia

If symptomatic or severe anemia: consider RBC transfusion per symptoms/hemodynamics and local thresholds; coordinate with hematology
Review exposures that can cause/accentuate sideroblastic anemia (alcohol, lead, isoniazid, linezolid) and eliminate if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and reticulocyte count in 4-6 weeks after starting pyridoxine; earlier (1-2 weeks) if symptomatic or severe anemia

Do not start iron unless iron deficiency is proven; discuss iron overload risk

Discuss family implications and potential genetic counseling if confirmed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit anemia symptoms, duration, bleeding history, diet, alcohol/toxin exposure, medications (incl. isoniazid/linezolid), family history (X-linked), prior labs, and red-flag symptoms to guide workup and urgency.

Interpret results to support suspected X-linked sideroblastic anemia (microcytosis with inappropriately low retic response; iron studies often normal/high; smear may show dimorphic RBCs/basophilic stippling) and to rule out iron deficiency and thalassemia.

State working diagnosis (suspected X-linked sideroblastic anemia) and differential; clarify that confirmation may require genetic testing (ALAS2) and/or bone marrow findings (ring sideroblasts) via hematology.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms, outline next tests/referral, and address concerns, safety-net symptoms, and family/genetic implications.
Critical safety check before prescribing pyridoxine/folic acid or any other medication.
Check for interactions (non-critical) with current meds/supplements, including high-dose B6 considerations and any concurrent therapies.
Initiate a trial of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) and folic acid; explicitly avoid empiric iron unless iron deficiency is proven; consider hematology referral for dosing/monitoring and confirmation.
Arrange repeat labs and clinical reassessment in weeks to assess response; coordinate hematology referral and genetic counseling if confirmed/suspected.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 12-year-old anxious 7th-grade student. Speak vaguely and a little worried; let your parent do most talking. Start by saying your knees and ankles keep getting puffy and hurting for months, worse in the mornings with stiffness that takes a while to loosen up. Mention on-and-off limping, getting tired and not wanting to play, and sometimes a low fever when joints act up.

Only if asked, add: wrists/fingers hurt when writing or holding things; swelling comes in waves; pain wakes you 1–2 nights/week during bad flares; occasional belly pain/poor appetite; a few episodes of red, light-sensitive eyes that went away; a little unintentional weight loss.

Do not volunteer: bedwetting during painful fever nights, feeling down/irritable, being shorter/not growing well, jaw soreness chewing, hip/back stiffness after sitting, faint rash with fevers.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s a blood problem or “periodic fever,” maybe immune issues from mouth sores, or just growing pains. Minimize symptoms due to cost; prefer cheap OTC meds and fewer visits. If clinician shows empathy, you relax slightly, answer more, but still worry about money and missing school/work.

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1
differentials: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis due to LACC1 (FAMIN) deficiency (monogenic systemic autoinflammatory disease with chronic inflammatory arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Systemic autoinflammatory disease with chronic polyarthritis (LACC1 deficiency)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling, pain, morning stiffness, limited range of motion) involving one or more joints for ≥6 weeks
Inflammatory markers elevated during active disease (ESR and/or CRP)
CBC may show anemia of inflammation and/or thrombocytosis consistent with chronic inflammation
Iron studies pattern consistent with inflammation (ferritin normal/high with low/normal serum iron) rather than primary iron deficiency
Known pathogenic LACC1 variant OR prior documented diagnosis consistent with LACC1 deficiency (if available in history)

No lab pattern suggesting cyclic hematopoiesis (no recurrent periodic neutropenia on serial CBCs) and no strong evidence for syndromic sideroblastic anemia/B-cell immunodeficiency syndrome (e.g., microcytic anemia with iron overload, recurrent severe infections)

expected_lab_results.ESR: Elevated during active arthritis (often >20–30 mm/hr; may be higher with flares)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Elevated during active inflammation (above reference range)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal to mildly low (anemia of chronic inflammation possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal to mildly low/normal (typically normocytic; microcytosis would prompt alternate anemia evaluation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible); not cyclic severe neutropenia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to elevated (acute-phase reactant; may be increased with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Iron: Low to low-normal (consistent with inflammation-related iron sequestration)

medications: Etodolac 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID with food (max 1000 mg/day) for symptom control
Naproxen 10 mg/kg/dose PO BID with food as an alternative NSAID if etodolac ineffective or not tolerated (max 1000 mg/day)
Methotrexate 10–15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly for persistent active arthritis; titrate up to 20–25 mg/m^2/week as tolerated
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO weekly) while on methotrexate
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 SC every 2 weeks (max 40 mg every 2 weeks) if inadequate response to methotrexate and ongoing active disease
Prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO short course for severe flare/bridging therapy with plan to taper; consider intra-articular triamcinolone for limited-joint disease
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology follow-up and treat-to-target monitoring of disease activity
Physical and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, function, and joint protection
Exercise plan emphasizing low-impact aerobic activity and stretching; encourage regular activity as tolerated
Heat/cold modalities for symptom relief and pacing of activities during flares
Ophthalmology screening for uveitis per JIA risk profile and at diagnosis (frequency individualized)
Vaccination review: keep routine immunizations up to date; avoid live vaccines if starting biologic or significant immunosuppression
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4–6 weeks after NSAID optimization or after starting DMARD; then every 1–3 months until controlled, with labs every 4–8 weeks while on methotrexate

must_include: Condition is chronic inflammatory arthritis related to a genetic immune/inflammation pathway (LACC1/FAMIN) and can cause recurrent or persistent joint inflammation
Goal is to control inflammation early to prevent joint damage, pain, and disability
Discuss why differentials are less likely based on available labs (no clear cyclic neutropenia pattern; iron studies not consistent with sideroblastic anemia/iron overload)
Stepwise approach: NSAID for pain/inflammation, then DMARD (methotrexate) if persistent activity, then biologic if inadequate response
Expected timeline: NSAIDs help within days; methotrexate takes ~6–12 weeks; biologics often within weeks
Key medication risks and monitoring: NSAID GI/renal risk; methotrexate liver/blood count monitoring and folic acid; biologic infection risk and screening/vaccines
Review amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash) and clarify reaction severity; ensure it is documented
Infection precautions if immunosuppressive therapy is used (fever plan, when to seek care)
Shared decision-making about escalation to DMARD/biologic based on symptoms, function, and inflammatory markers
Importance of adherence, regular activity/therapy, and maintaining joint range of motion
NSAID administration with food and avoidance of additional OTC NSAIDs unless advised
Follow-up plan and need for repeat labs to track inflammation and medication safety

Retrieve results to support diagnosis: decrement on RNS or increased jitter on SFEMG; genetic confirmation of glycosylation-defect CMS; assess for red flags suggesting alternative diagnoses or coexisting autoimmune MG.
Make and communicate diagnosis of congenital myasthenic syndrome with glycosylation defect; explicitly differentiate from autoimmune myasthenia gravis and note implications for treatment.
Critical safety step before prescribing: verify allergies/intolerances to proposed agents (e.g., pyridostigmine, amifampridine) and prior adverse reactions.

Critical safety step before prescribing: review for interacting drugs and drugs that may worsen neuromuscular transmission (e.g., aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides in some cases, magnesium, beta-blockers, certain antiarrhythmics, botulinum toxin); assess QT/seizure-threshold lowering combinations if considering amifampridine.

Initiate or adjust symptomatic therapy (typically pyridostigmine; consider amifampridine if indicated/available and appropriate for subtype), with titration plan, maximum dose limits, and monitoring instructions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (inherited NMJ disorder, fluctuating symptoms, EMG/genetics, differs from MG and immunotherapies usually not helpful), explain treatment (goals, how to take meds, side effects, seizure/palpitations warning for amifampridine, urgent care triggers), address concerns (med review, genetic counseling, follow-up expectations), and lifestyle guidance (pacing, infection plan, anesthesia precautions).
Arrange follow-up to assess response, adverse effects, and need for dose adjustment; provide earlier review if bulbar/respiratory symptoms or rapid worsening.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, questions answered, and follow-up plan in place.

Do not volunteer hidden issues unless specifically asked (memory gaps/repeating questions, unsteady walking, resting hand tremor, a couple bladder accidents, sounds too loud). Misattribute illness to food poisoning, flu/COVID, migraine, or heatstroke. Withhold: no travel vaccines; don’t mention alcohol/stimulant pills unless repeatedly pressed—then admit reluctantly and ask not to write it down. Express strong worry about hospital costs; parent suspects referral is for profit and asks for cheaper local injections. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly and answer more.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Japanese encephalitis
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with neurologic dysfunction (e.g., altered mental status, seizures, focal deficits, signs of encephalitis)
Epidemiologic risk: travel/residence in JE-endemic area with mosquito exposure (often rural/agricultural), lack of vaccination or incomplete vaccination
No alternative explanation on initial assessment; urgent consideration of other treatable encephalitides (especially HSV) while awaiting confirmatory testing
Supportive labs may show systemic inflammation; definitive diagnosis requires JE virus-specific IgM in CSF/serum (or PCR early in illness where available)

expected_lab_results.CBC Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly low
expected_lab_results.Differential Neutrophils: Normal to mildly increased early (relative neutrophilia possible)
expected_lab_results.Differential Lymphocytes: Relative lymphopenia early; may normalize later
expected_lab_results.CRP: Mildly elevated (often <50 mg/L); very high values raise concern for bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Serum Sodium: May be low (hyponatremia possible due to SIADH in CNS infection)

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h
Levetiracetam 1000 mg IV/PO q12h (if seizures or high seizure risk)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain (max 3 g/day typical adult)
0.9% Sodium chloride IV titrated to euvolemia (or hypertonic saline per protocol if symptomatic hyponatremia)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU-level monitoring if altered mental status, seizures, respiratory compromise, or concern for increased intracranial pressure
Airway protection as needed; aspiration precautions; head-of-bed elevation
Seizure precautions; continuous or frequent neurologic checks
Manage suspected increased intracranial pressure per protocol (avoid hypotonic fluids; consider osmotherapy if indicated)
Obtain confirmatory testing: CSF studies including JE virus IgM (and meningoencephalitis panel where available); neuroimaging and EEG as clinically indicated

Start empiric coverage for other treatable causes of encephalitis/meningoencephalitis per local protocol (e.g., add antibiotics if bacterial meningitis cannot be excluded) while awaiting results; de-escalate when ruled out
Infection prevention counseling: mosquito bite avoidance; public health notification per jurisdiction if confirmed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after initiation of empiric therapy and stabilization; daily inpatient reassessment with neurologic monitoring; post-discharge follow-up in 1-2 weeks and neuro-rehabilitation referral as needed.

must_include: Encephalitis means inflammation/infection of the brain causing fever and neurologic symptoms
Japanese encephalitis is mosquito-borne and linked to exposure in endemic regions
Diagnosis is confirmed with specific testing (often JE IgM in CSF/serum), but treatment and monitoring must start immediately because of severity
No proven curative antiviral for Japanese encephalitis; care is mainly supportive (brain swelling, seizures, breathing, fluids/electrolytes)
Acyclovir is started empirically to cover HSV encephalitis until it is excluded because HSV is treatable and time-sensitive
Potential need for ICU monitoring and why (seizures, airway protection, brain swelling)
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and potential neurologic complications
Explain warning signs requiring urgent escalation (worsening confusion, seizures, trouble breathing)
Review medication safety, including allergy history (amoxicillin rash) and that planned meds are not penicillins
Mosquito avoidance measures (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets, avoiding dusk/dawn exposure)
Vaccination counseling for future travel to endemic areas (JE vaccine when indicated)
Rehabilitation and cognitive/functional recovery planning if deficits persist

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected encephalitis and Japanese encephalitis exposure (timeline, fever, headache, confusion, seizures, focal deficits), travel/residence in endemic area, mosquito exposure, vaccination status, and alternative causes (HSV risk, immunocompromise).

Screen for red flags and immediate stabilization needs (airway/breathing, rapidly worsening mental status, recurrent seizures), and obtain medication history including prior reactions and current meds/substances.

Order initial evaluation for encephalitis/meningitis and organ function prior to antivirals/supportive care: CBC, CMP (renal/hepatic), glucose, coagulation as needed, blood cultures, lactate if ill-appearing; consider pregnancy test when applicable; and plan neuroimaging prior to LP if indicated.

Order encephalitis-specific diagnostics: lumbar puncture with CSF cell count/diff, protein, glucose, Gram stain/culture, CSF HSV PCR, and JE IgM (CSF and/or serum); consider additional arboviral testing per region and season.
Review available results to support encephalitis diagnosis, assess renal function for acyclovir dosing, and identify alternative diagnoses requiring different therapy.

Synthesize clinical syndrome (acute febrile encephalopathy) and exposure risk to diagnose suspected encephalitis with high concern for Japanese encephalitis while acknowledging confirmation requires JE IgM testing; maintain differential including HSV encephalitis.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergy history (e.g., amoxicillin rash) and assess relevance to planned medications (acyclovir, antiseizure meds, supportive meds).

Noncritical safety check before prescribing: evaluate interactions, especially nephrotoxic combinations affecting acyclovir risk (e.g., NSAIDs, aminoglycosides) and sedatives with altered mental status.

Initiate empiric, time-sensitive therapy and supportive care: start IV acyclovir for possible HSV encephalitis; manage seizures if present; maintain fluids/electrolytes; avoid hypotonic fluids; consider measures for increased ICP; and arrange ICU-level monitoring when indicated.

Critical contraindication check before ordering procedures/therapies: ensure LP safety prerequisites (e.g., imaging first if focal deficits/papilledema/severely altered mental status; check platelets/INR if bleeding risk) and renal function monitoring for acyclovir.

Communicate diagnosis, testing, and treatment plan clearly: explain encephalitis, JE mosquito-borne exposure, need for immediate supportive care, empiric acyclovir rationale, and ICU monitoring reasons; address prognosis uncertainty and warning signs; provide mosquito avoidance and future vaccination counseling.

Arrange ongoing monitoring and disposition: ICU/step-down admission for neurologic checks, seizure precautions, and follow-up of CSF/serology results; plan rehabilitation/cognitive recovery support if deficits persist.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, orders, treatment initiation, and patient/family communication milestones are completed.

patient.instructions:

must_include: RA is an autoimmune inflammatory arthritis that can damage joints if untreated

Methotrexate is a first-line disease-modifying therapy to prevent joint damage; it is taken weekly (not daily)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit RA-focused history (inflammatory joint symptoms, pattern, duration), functional impact, red flags (infection, cardiopulmonary), pregnancy intent/contraception, prior meds and response, comorbidities, and complete medication list.

Order diagnostic and baseline safety labs for suspected RA and DMARD initiation: RF, anti-CCP, ESR/CRP; CBC with differential, CMP (AST/ALT), creatinine/eGFR; hepatitis B/C and TB screening per local protocol; pregnancy test if applicable; consider baseline lipids if planning JAK inhibitor later.
Review results to support diagnosis and ensure baseline safety prior to methotrexate; identify contraindications (cytopenias, significant transaminitis, renal impairment, pregnancy, hepatitis).

Make/confirm working diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis based on inflammatory symptoms/exam plus supportive serologies/inflammatory markers; document differential and triggers to reconsider (atypical features such as lung symptoms).

Critical before prescribing: check interactions with current meds (including choline magnesium trisalicylate and any NSAIDs, anticoagulants, TMP-SMX, alcohol use) and planned methotrexate/prednisone/PPIs as relevant.

Initiate first-line DMARD if no contraindications: methotrexate weekly (not daily) plus folic acid; consider short prednisone bridge for symptom control; avoid/limit NSAIDs that worsen GI symptoms and consider gastroprotection if needed.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, monitoring, infection precautions, reproductive safety, lifestyle guidance, and medication safety (avoid duplicate salicylates/NSAIDs).
Arrange follow-up to assess response and adverse effects; set lab monitoring cadence (CBC/LFT/Cr at baseline and periodically after start) and escalation plan if inadequate control.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) due to LACC1 (FAMIN) gene defect (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Systemic autoinflammatory disease with chronic inflammatory arthritis
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling and/or pain with morning stiffness)
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) during active disease
Seronegative autoimmune markers (RF negative and anti-CCP negative) consistent with many JIA phenotypes and LACC1-associated disease
CBC may show anemia of chronic inflammation and/or thrombocytosis during active inflammation
Known or suspected LACC1 mutation with compatible clinical phenotype (if available from history/genetics)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low-normal to mildly decreased (anemia of chronic disease may be present during active inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal (normocytic) or mildly low if concurrent iron deficiency; often normocytic in inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible during flares)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis common with inflammation)

medications: Meloxicam 0.125 mg/kg PO once daily (max 7.5 mg/day) with food
Naproxen 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1000 mg/day) as alternative NSAID if meloxicam inadequate or not tolerated
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (max 25 mg/week) with folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg once weekly the day after MTX) for persistent active arthritis despite NSAID
Triamcinolone hexacetonide intra-articular injection (dose per joint/weight) for active synovitis in 1-2 joints as a steroid-sparing option
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 SC every other week (max 40 mg) or Etanercept 0.8 mg/kg SC weekly (max 50 mg) if inadequate response to methotrexate or for severe disease (per pediatric rheumatology guidance)
non_pharmacological: Referral to pediatric rheumatology for disease-modifying therapy plan and monitoring
Physical and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, and function; home exercise program
Screen for and manage uveitis risk with ophthalmology slit-lamp exams per JIA schedule (frequency based on subtype/risk factors)
Vaccination review: ensure age-appropriate immunizations; avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose immunosuppression
School/activity accommodations during flares; encourage regular low-impact activity as tolerated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms, joint exam, and ESR/CRP in 4-6 weeks after NSAID initiation/adjustment; sooner (2-4 weeks) if worsening, systemic symptoms, medication adverse effects, or functional decline

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; symptoms can flare and improve over time
LACC1-associated disease is a genetic form that can behave like JIA and often has negative RF/anti-CCP tests
The goal is to control inflammation early to prevent joint damage and preserve growth and function
NSAIDs (e.g., meloxicam) help pain and stiffness but may not fully control inflammation; escalation to DMARDs/biologics may be needed
How to take meloxicam (once daily with food; avoid combining with other NSAIDs unless instructed)
Key adverse effects and when to seek care: stomach pain/bleeding, black stools, reduced urine output, swelling, shortness of breath, rash
If methotrexate/biologics are started: need for regular lab monitoring, infection precautions, and vaccine considerations
Allergy history: amoxicillin caused hives; confirm no NSAID allergy and discuss signs of allergic reaction
Discuss expectations for symptom improvement timeline and the plan if symptoms persist
Shared decision-making about steroid injections vs systemic therapy and balancing benefits/risks
Maintain gentle daily activity and stretching; avoid prolonged immobilization
Use heat/cold and pacing strategies for flares; prioritize sleep
When to return urgently: fever with severe joint pain, inability to bear weight, red hot swollen joint, vision changes/eye pain, GI bleeding symptoms

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, joints involved, severity, functional limits, red flags (fever, inability to bear weight, hot swollen joint), ocular symptoms (uveitis), prior treatments/response, and patient/parent goals.

Clarify past medical history and risk factors relevant to NSAID/DMARD safety: GI ulcer/bleeding, kidney disease, liver disease, asthma with NSAID sensitivity, cardiovascular history, pregnancy possibility (if adolescent), infection history/exposures.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm amoxicillin allergy details and verify no NSAID allergy; document reaction type and severity.
Order baseline labs to assess inflammation and medication safety and to support management decisions (e.g., CBC, CMP/creatinine, AST/ALT, ESR/CRP; consider urinalysis).
Review lab results to ensure NSAID/DMARD safety (renal/hepatic function, cytopenias) and assess inflammatory activity.
Confirm/communicate juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect; distinguish from septic arthritis/other urgent causes using history, exam context, and red flags.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen current meds/supplements for NSAID interactions (e.g., other NSAIDs, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, steroids, SSRIs, ACEi/ARB/diuretics).
Start symptomatic anti-inflammatory therapy (e.g., meloxicam) if no contraindications; provide dosing instructions and precautions; consider escalation plan if inadequate control.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment options (NSAIDs vs DMARDs/biologics, steroid injections), adverse effects/when to seek care, infection/vaccine considerations if immunosuppressants, and lifestyle/flare management.
Arrange follow-up to reassess pain, stiffness, function, adverse effects, and lab monitoring; ensure ophthalmology screening plan for uveitis risk if applicable.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety net instructions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old female project manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “I just can’t sleep at night, and it’s been going on for months.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you lie awake 1–2 hours before sleep, wake frequently and can’t get back to sleep, feel tired/foggy next day, and that your dad and a sibling have similar insomnia.

If asked, say it happens 4–5 nights/week, even on weekends/vacations; you’re more irritable and unfocused at work; you avoid naps; no choking/shortness of breath awakenings; no restless legs sensations; no significant pain; you drink coffee but stop early afternoon.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: you spend lots of time awake in bed scrolling/watching shows, worry as bedtime approaches, check the clock often, sometimes use a glass of wine, and have been working late with a later bedtime.

Be reluctant about: frequent OTC sleep-aid use and occasionally taking a friend’s sleep pill—admit only if asked nonjudgmentally.

Express misconceptions: your mind feels “wired” at night with late evening energy; melatonin didn’t help so you worry about hormones.

Raise cost concerns about sleep studies and CBT-I. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more honest.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insomnia disorder (chronic insomnia)

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months for chronic insomnia (or specify acute if <3 months)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep

Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder, substance/medication effect, or medical/psychiatric condition

non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) (in-person or digital)
Sleep hygiene: consistent sleep/wake schedule; limit naps; avoid caffeine after noon; avoid nicotine and alcohol near bedtime; reduce evening screen/bright light; wind-down routine
Stimulus control: bed only for sleep/sex; leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes; return when sleepy

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks (earlier if worsening, safety issues, or concern for another sleep disorder); then adjust plan and consider referral to sleep medicine/behavioral health if not improving by 6-8 weeks

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief (seconds to 2 minutes) episodes of severe, electric shock-like or stabbing unilateral facial pain in trigeminal nerve distribution
Pain triggered by innocuous stimuli (allodynia) such as light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, or cold air
Stereotyped attacks with pain-free intervals and no prominent cranial autonomic symptoms
Normal neurologic exam (no objective sensory loss) and no red flags suggesting secondary cause

Differentials considered: cluster headache (typically 15–180 min attacks with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and restlessness) and familial headache syndromes; clinical pattern favors trigeminal neuralgia

expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Within normal range at baseline (monitor for hyponatremia after starting carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine)
expected_lab_results.CMP - AST: Within normal range at baseline (monitor for hepatotoxicity with carbamazepine)
expected_lab_results.CMP - ALT: Within normal range at baseline (monitor for hepatotoxicity with carbamazepine)
expected_lab_results.Inflammatory markers - ESR: Normal (helps argue against giant cell arteritis or other inflammatory etiologies)

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily; titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to pain control (typical 400-1200 mg/day in divided doses); monitor CBC, CMP (Na, AST/ALT) and for rash/sedation
If carbamazepine not tolerated/contraindicated: Oxcarbazepine 150 mg PO twice daily; titrate to 600-1800 mg/day; monitor sodium for hyponatremia
non_pharmacological: Order/arrange MRI brain with and without contrast focused on trigeminal nerve/cerebellopontine angle to exclude secondary causes (tumor, MS, vascular compression) if first presentation, atypical features, or age <40
Identify and minimize triggers (gentle oral hygiene, avoid cold wind/air on face, soft foods during flares)
Discuss procedural options if medication-refractory or intolerant (microvascular decompression; percutaneous rhizotomy; stereotactic radiosurgery) via neurology/neurosurgery referral
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for response and adverse effects; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if worsening pain, new neurologic deficits, rash, fever, or severe dizziness/somnolence

First-line therapy is an anticonvulsant (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine), taken daily and titrated

Need for lab monitoring (CBC and liver enzymes; sodium especially with oxcarbazepine) and follow-up to adjust dose

Do not stop medication abruptly without medical advice; keep a pain/trigger diary to guide titration

Do not mention unless directly asked: cuts/scrapes getting red easily, gums bleeding/sore when brushing during sick weeks, mild belly aches/low appetite, and that this has been happening for months.

Resist bringing up painful sores around your bottom; if asked directly and privately, admit embarrassedly that they come and go.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe allergies,” “Could it be COVID/flu?” “Maybe reflux because my throat burns.” If shown empathy, relax, answer more clearly, and accept reassurance. Parent asks about cost, fewer blood tests, and bundling visits.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cyclic neutropenia (cyclic hematopoiesis) with acute respiratory tract infection
acceptable_alternatives: Cyclic hematopoiesis presenting as cyclic neutropenia with intercurrent upper respiratory infection

required_evidence: History of recurrent infections occurring at regular intervals (typically every ~21 days) consistent with cyclic hematopoiesis/cyclic neutropenia

Respiratory tract infection symptoms (e.g., cough, sore throat, congestion) with supportive inflammatory marker pattern (CRP may be elevated)
No alternative explanation for neutropenia (e.g., new offending medication, acute leukemia/aplastic process suggested by pancytopenia)

expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Low during nadir; may be normal between cycles if prior data available
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Low during nadir (often <0.5 x10^9/L in severe episodes); key diagnostic abnormality
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if broader cyclic hematopoiesis affects erythropoiesis
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Usually normal; may be mildly low paralleling hemoglobin
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal; may show mild cyclic variation but not persistently low
expected_lab_results.Absolute monocyte count: Often relatively increased during neutropenic nadir (relative monocytosis)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Typically normal; may vary if cyclic hematopoiesis involves erythroid line
expected_lab_results.C-reactive protein (CRP): Normal to elevated depending on severity/etiology of respiratory infection; higher if bacterial infection or systemic inflammation

medications: Filgrastim (G-CSF) 5 mcg/kg subcutaneous once daily during neutropenic nadir until ANC recovery (per hematology plan)
Acetaminophen 650 mg oral every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day if no liver disease)

Doxycycline 100 mg oral twice daily for 5-7 days if bacterial respiratory infection is suspected and outpatient management is appropriate (avoid beta-lactam due to allergy); adjust based on local guidance and clinical severity
non_pharmacological: If fever (≥38.0°C) with ANC <0.5 x10^9/L or appears systemically unwell: urgent ED evaluation for possible neutropenic fever and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics

Coordinate/confirm hematology follow-up for cyclic neutropenia management and individualized G-CSF schedule; consider serial CBCs to document cycling pattern if not already established
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Same day/within 24 hours if fever, worsening symptoms, or ANC <0.5 x10^9/L; otherwise follow up in 48-72 hours to reassess symptoms and review CBC/ANC trend, with hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks

minimal_paths.description: Mild URI symptoms, no fever/red flags, ANC not severely low; supportive care with close follow-up and safety net.

steps: ASK (bacterial criteria + red flags + neutropenia cycle + meds/allergy)

EDUCATE (milestones; emphasize urgent evaluation if fever develops)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 52-year-old self-employed hairdresser. You are worried but evasive and vague; you minimize and change topics. Start with: “I can’t stop coughing and I’m short of breath, and my mouth and eyes have been insanely dry.” If asked “anything else,” volunteer: gritty sand-like dry eyes, dry mouth needing constant sips to swallow, dry cough ~3 weeks, winded on one flight of stairs, more tired.

Only reveal if directly asked: low-grade evening fevers, chest tightness with deep breaths, sticky saliva/trouble with crackers, hoarse by day’s end, waking up coughing, mild morning hand/wrist aches, glands under jaw, frequent eye drops/burning in wind, heartburn/sour taste.

Keep hidden unless specifically probed: more cavities, dry nose/nosebleeds, taste changes, unintentional weight loss, fingers turning white/numb in cold/stress, scratchy throat, mouth sores, dry itchy skin.

Resist/deflect sexual dryness and urine leakage unless asked gently; show embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: think it’s infection/asthma/tumor; mention yellow-green mucus, one scary “pneumonia” day, brief wheeze/whistle; admit trying 2 days of leftover antibiotics. Emphasize cost concerns; assume tests/specialists are optional; ration meds and prefer OTC supplements.

If clinician is empathetic, soften, give more detail, but stay somewhat guarded.

Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Pilocarpine 5 mg PO three times daily
Artificial tears (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) 1-2 drops OU four times daily PRN
Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily
confounders: Anticholinergic medication use (e.g., intermittent diphenhydramine for insomnia) worsening sicca symptoms
Perimenopausal status contributing to vaginal dryness and fatigue
Chronic GERD treated intermittently with antihistamines/PPIs, which can contribute to dry mouth and dental caries risk
Prednisolone therapy potentially lowering inflammatory markers and masking disease activity

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sjogren syndrome with interstitial lung disease (Sjogren-associated ILD; consider organizing pneumonia pattern if acute/subacute infiltrates)
acceptable_alternatives: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (organizing pneumonia pattern) in the setting of Sjogren syndrome
Acute interstitial pneumonia (if fulminant ARDS-like presentation and diffuse alveolar damage suspected)
required_evidence: Known or strongly suspected Sjogren syndrome (sicca symptoms and/or positive anti-SSA/Ro or anti-SSB/La, or minor salivary gland biopsy if available)
Respiratory symptoms consistent with ILD/OP (progressive dyspnea and/or cough; hypoxemia if present)
Chest CT showing interstitial changes (e.g., ground-glass opacities, reticulation, traction bronchiectasis) and/or organizing pneumonia features (patchy peripheral/peribronchovascular consolidation)
Exclusion of infection as primary driver (no convincing bacterial pneumonia features; cultures/viral testing considered; lack of neutrophilic leukocytosis or procalcitonin elevation if obtained)
Consider bronchoscopy/BAL or biopsy if diagnosis unclear or to exclude malignancy/endobronchial tumor when imaging suggests airway lesion

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal or mildly elevated; significant neutrophilic leukocytosis would increase concern for bacterial pneumonia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be normal or mild anemia of chronic disease
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal; thrombocytosis can be reactive with inflammation/infection
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal unless comorbid renal disease; establish baseline before immunosuppression escalation
expected_lab_results.CMP - eGFR: Typically preserved; reduced eGFR would affect antibiotic choices/dosing if infection treated
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be normal; mild hyponatremia can occur with systemic illness
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: May be low-normal or low if on systemic steroids; monitor for hypokalemia
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Usually normal; may track with sodium changes

medications: Prednisolone 0.75 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 60 mg/day) for 2-4 weeks, then taper based on clinical and radiographic response
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily (GI protection while on high-dose steroids if risk factors present)
Calcium carbonate 500 mg elemental PO daily plus cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 800-1000 IU PO daily (bone protection while on prolonged steroids)
non_pharmacological: Urgent high-resolution CT chest (if not already done) to characterize ILD/OP pattern and exclude mass/airway lesion
Pulse oximetry at rest and with exertion; supplemental oxygen if SpO2 < 88-90% or symptomatic hypoxemia
Pulmonary function tests (spirometry, lung volumes, DLCO) for baseline and monitoring once stable
Infectious evaluation before escalating immunosuppression if clinically suspected (sputum culture, viral testing; consider bronchoscopy/BAL if severe or unclear)

Rheumatology and pulmonology co-management; consider steroid-sparing agent (e.g., mycophenolate mofetil or azathioprine) if recurrent/progressive ILD or steroid dependence (initiation typically specialist-led)
Vaccination review (influenza, pneumococcal) and smoking cessation counseling if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours if acutely unwell or hypoxemic; otherwise clinic follow-up in 1-2 weeks with symptom check and steroid taper plan; repeat imaging in 6-12 weeks to document response

must_include: Lung involvement can occur in Sjogren syndrome and may cause inflammation/scarring (interstitial lung disease) or an organizing pneumonia pattern
Why infection and other differentials (staphylococcal pneumonia, acute interstitial pneumonia, tumor) are being considered and what findings would shift the diagnosis
Need for CT chest and possibly further tests (PFTs, sputum/BAL) to confirm pattern and rule out infection/malignancy
Steroids are used to reduce lung inflammation; expected timeline for improvement and the need for a gradual taper
Key steroid adverse effects (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, infection risk, GI irritation, bone loss) and when to seek help
Plan for monitoring response (symptoms, oxygenation, imaging, PFTs) and possibility of adding steroid-sparing immunosuppression if needed
Review allergies: sulfonamide antibiotics caused urticarial rash; penicillin childhood reaction is unclear—avoid empiric beta-lactams unless evaluated or benefits outweigh risks
Safety-net for red flags: worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, hemoptysis, fever, confusion, SpO2 drop—seek urgent care
Discuss infection risk while on prednisolone and importance of early evaluation for fevers/new cough
Bone protection while on steroids (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise as tolerated)
Avoid sick contacts when possible; hand hygiene; keep vaccinations up to date
If oxygen is prescribed, safe use instructions and activity pacing

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timeline, severity, and red flags; clarify Sjogren history, prior lung disease, infection exposures, and current meds.
Targeted ROS and risk assessment: fever, purulent sputum, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, weight loss, aspiration risk, TB/fungal risks, smoking/vaping, occupational exposures.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm sulfonamide urticarial rash and clarify penicillin reaction details.
Order initial evaluation for pulmonary process and infection/inflammation: CBC with diff, CMP, CRP/ESR; consider procalcitonin; baseline glucose if steroids anticipated.
Order pulmonary workup tests as indicated: pulse oximetry (rest/exertion), ABG if hypoxemic, sputum culture/Gram stain if productive, viral testing if appropriate.
Order imaging and functional testing: CT chest (HRCT preferred) and PFTs; consider CXR as initial screen if CT not immediately available.
Synthesize Sjogren-related lung involvement differential (ILD/OP) vs infection (e.g., staphylococcal pneumonia), acute interstitial pneumonia, malignancy; assign working diagnosis and uncertainty.
Review returned labs/imaging/oxygenation to determine likelihood of infection vs inflammatory lung disease and severity to guide treatment and disposition.
Critical safety check before ordering any potentially harmful tests/therapies (e.g., CT contrast if used; ensure renal function/contrast allergy; pregnancy status if relevant).

Critical safety check before prescribing steroids/antibiotics: uncontrolled infection concern, severe hyperglycemia, GI bleed risk, osteoporosis risk, psychiatric history, active TB/strongyloides risk where relevant.
Non-critical but expected: check interactions for planned prednisolone and any antibiotics/PPIs/bone protection meds with the patient’s current medication list.

Initiate appropriate therapy based on results: prednisolone for suspected OP/ILD when infection reasonably excluded/covered; avoid beta-lactams if penicillin allergy unclear unless benefits outweigh risks; avoid sulfonamide antibiotics given urticaria history.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis/differentials and need for CT/PFTs; explain steroid plan/taper, adverse effects, monitoring; address allergies/infection risk; lifestyle/bone protection/vaccines/oxygen safety; safety-net red flags.
Arrange timely reassessment (days to 1–2 weeks depending on severity), repeat oxygenation, symptom check, review imaging/PFTs; plan escalation (pulmonology/rheumatology, BAL/biopsy) if not improving.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, follow-up plan, and return precautions.

Agent discusses infection risk on prednisolone and advises early evaluation for fevers/new cough.

Agent provides lifestyle education: bone protection (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise as tolerated), avoid sick contacts/hand hygiene, vaccination updates; if oxygen prescribed, safe use and pacing.
Agent’s plan is severity-appropriate (e.g., hypoxemia/rapid progression triggers urgent evaluation/hospitalization rather than outpatient management).
Agent avoids unsafe antibiotic choices given allergy history and avoids immunosuppression escalation without appropriate infection exclusion or specialist involvement.

minimal_paths.description: Suspected Sjogren-associated organizing pneumonia/ILD without severe hypoxemia; infection reasonably excluded; outpatient steroid initiation with close follow-up.
must_collect: Sjogren history and prior pulmonary involvement
exposure risks (sick contacts, travel, TB/fungal, aspiration, smoking/vaping)
steps: ASK focused HPI/ROS and severity + red flags
ORDER_LAB CT chest (HRCT) and PFTs; sputum studies if productive
DIAGNOSE working dx: Sjogren lung involvement (OP/ILD) vs infection/malignancy with rationale
GET_RESULTS and reassess likelihood of infection and severity
contraindication_check before ordering/therapy as relevant (e.g., contrast risk; pregnancy if applicable)
PRESCRIBE prednisolone with taper plan (and supportive measures as indicated)
EDUCATE diagnosis/differentials, steroid effects, monitoring, allergies, safety-net, lifestyle/bone protection/vaccines
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP within 3–7 days (earlier if borderline oxygenation) and plan repeat assessment/imaging/PFT review

minimal_paths.description: Presentation concerning for infection or unclear; prioritize microbiologic evaluation and avoid/limit steroids until infection addressed; escalate care if hypoxemic.
must_collect: SpO2 trend and work of breathing
steps: ASK focused infection and severity assessment
ORDER_LAB CBC/CMP/CRP(±procalcitonin), blood cultures if febrile, sputum culture/viral testing as indicated
ORDER_LAB imaging (CXR and/or CT chest) and oxygenation assessment
DIAGNOSE: infection vs inflammatory lung disease; document uncertainty
PRESCRIBE appropriate non-beta-lactam empiric therapy if bacterial pneumonia suspected and allergy limits options; defer or use cautious steroid approach only with clear indication/specialist input
EDUCATE about differentials, need for CT/PFTs/BAL if needed, infection risk, and urgent red flags
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP or urgent referral/ED transfer if hypoxemic/unstable

acceptable_variants.description: Pulmonology-forward diagnostic pathway: early CT chest + PFTs ordered, with planned bronchoscopy/BAL if CT suggests OP/ILD but infection/malignancy remains possible.
steps: DIAGNOSE differential emphasizing OP/ILD vs infection vs malignancy
PRESCRIBE steroids if infection reasonably excluded; otherwise plan BAL/targeted cultures first
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP with pulmonology and repeat testing plan

acceptable_variants.description: Inpatient escalation variant for severe disease: hypoxemia or rapid progression triggers ED/hospital admission for oxygen, expedited CT, cultures, and specialist evaluation prior to immunosuppression escalation.
steps: ASK and identify instability (SpO2 drop, severe dyspnea, confusion, chest pain, hemoptysis)
ORDER_LAB urgent oxygenation assessment + baseline labs and cultures if febrile
ORDER_LAB urgent imaging (CXR/CT) and consider ABG
PRESCRIBE oxygen and hospital-appropriate meds per findings; cautious steroid initiation only when infection addressed/ruled out
EDUCATE red flags, infection risk, and hospital plan
